segments.s774-g9: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase meaning "thus said" (word order: adverbial phrase + past verb). ماهوی is a proper name (the speaker) followed by two coordinated adjectives بدبخت و شوم describing him; the noun (name) comes before its adjectives in this poetic line.
segments.s774-g10: که introduces the content of the speech (that‑clause). ایران is the topic/subject, نبد is an archaic negative past form meaning "was not / did not exist" (equivalent to نبود), پیش از این = "before this," and خویش روم links a possessive/identifying phrase with روم ('Rome' or 'the Romans'); word order is subject + (negative) verb + temporal phrase + modifier.
segments.s774-g11: فرستاد is a simple past "he sent." تا here marks purpose/result ("so that"/"in order that"). هر که is a free relative "whoever/anyone who," and آن دخمه کرد literally "did that tomb" — کرد is the plain past of کردن meaning "made/did"; together: "he sent so that whoever had made that tomb..."
segments.s774-g12: هم is an additive particle "also/too." آنکس = "that person/that one." کزان is a fused prepositional relative meaning "from whom / from which." کار تیمار = "the affair/matter of care/treatment" (کار = deed/affair + تیمار = care). خورد here is intransitive past "befell/wasfalling on" (i.e., the matter befell/concerned him), so the phrase means "also that man from whom that matter of concern arose."
segments.s774-g13: بکشتند is past plural "they killed." The conjunction و links the second verb phrase تاراج کردند "they plundered/looted." مرز (the object) appears after the verbs and serves as the affected location/thing for these actions; the verb‑first order is common in epic poetry.
segments.s774-g14: چنین بود is a copular construction "thus was / such was." ماهوی را uses را as a topical/definite marker applying to the person Mahuy. کام و ارز are two coordinated nouns ("desire and value/wealth") that complete the clause: "Thus were for Mahuy desire and wealth" — idiomatically, "such were Mahuy's desire and riches."
segments.s774-g15: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial "after that / thereafter." بگرد is an imperative form meaning "turn/go about/search" (imperative singular or jussive), جهان is the object/topic "the world," and بنگرید is a polite/plural imperative "look/observe." The two imperatives together form a poetic exhortation: "go around the world and look."
segments.s774-g16: ز is a poetic preposition "from." تخم بزرگان = "the seed/offspring of the great ones (ancestors)." کسی را ندید is a negated past "he saw no one / he did not see anyone," where کسی with the negative expresses "no one" and را marks it as definite/object. Overall: "From the progeny of the great ones he saw no one."
segments.s774-g41: This is an imperative clause. 'جهاندیدگان' (the worldly-wise) is the definite object introduced by the object marker 'را' (here marking/foregrounding the group). 'همه' emphasizes totality. 'گرد کن' is an old/poetic imperative meaning 'assemble' or 'bring together' (literally 'make (them) around'). Word order (object + verb) is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s774-g42: 'زبان تیز گردان' is another imperative addressed to the speaker: 'make (your) tongue sharp' — i.e. sharpen your speech. 'گردان' here functions as an imperative causative/agentive ('cause to be / make'), and 'تیز' modifies 'زبان' = 'sharp, pointed'. 'به شیرین سخن' is an adverbial phrase 'with/in sweet speech' describing how the tongue should be sharpened (i.e. speak sharply but sweetly).
segments.s774-g43: 'چنین گوی' is an instruction 'say thus' (an archaic/poetic way to introduce reported speech). 'کاین' is the fused relative/pronominal particle 'که + این' meaning 'that this' or 'that which is this' and links to the following nouns 'تاج و انگشتری' (the crown and ring) as the content of what is to be said.
segments.s774-g44: This is a compact past clause with inverted order. 'مرا' (to me) is fronted (split in the provided word list), 'شاه' is the subject and 'داد' the past verb 'gave' (the king gave). 'از پی مهتری' is a prepositional phrase expressing motive or cause — 'because of / for the sake of مهتری' (here 'preeminence' or 'favour'). The construction is typical of epic diction where objects and particles may be separated for meter.
segments.s774-g45: 'چو دانست' = 'when he knew/when he learned' (چو as subordinating 'when'). 'کامد' is an archaic/past sense of 'came'/'arrived' (here describing the host's coming). 'ز ترکان سپاه' is a prepositional phrase 'an army/host from the Turks' and functions as the logical subject/agent of 'کامد'.
segments.s774-g46: The second 'چو' again means 'when'. 'شب تیره‌تر شد' = 'the night became darker' (تیره‌تر is comparative 'darker', شد is past 'became'). 'مرا خواند شاه' has inverted order: object 'مرا' then past verb 'خواند' ('summoned/called'), and finally the subject 'شاه' (the king). This inversion is normal in poetic narration.
segments.s774-g47: 'مرا گفت' = 'he said/told me' (past). What follows is reported content introduced by 'چون': 'چون خاست باد نبرد' is a compact, poetic clause meaning literally 'when the wind of battle rose' — 'باد نبرد' functions as a poetic compound 'the battle-wind' or 'storm of battle' and 'خاست' is past 'arose'. The whole clause is the content of what was said.
segments.s774-g48: This is a rhetorical/question tag: 'که داند به گیتی که بر کیست گرد' = 'for who knows in the world upon whom it will turn/fall'. 'که' introduces the clause 'that/who', 'داند' is present/past 'knows' (here rhetorical), 'به گیتی' = 'in the world', and the final sequence 'که بر کیست گرد' literally 'who it is upon whom (it) turns' — 'کی' = who, 'ست' = is (copula), and 'گرد' here has a verbal sense 'to turn/come upon' (poetic).
segments.s774-g65: یکی پهلوان گفت is a simple subject–verb clause: 'یکی پهلوان' = 'a certain champion' (indefinite subject) followed by the past verb گفت 'said'. کاین کار تست is a subordinate clause introduced by ک(=که) 'that'; کاین 'that/this' + کار 'affair/deed' functions as the subject of تست 'is a test' (here تست is a nominal predicate). Word order is typical Persian: Subject + Verb, and then the quoted/that-clause.
segments.s774-g66: سخن‌گر درستست‌گر نادرست is elliptical: سخن‌گر here refers to 'what the speaker says' or 'the speaker's speech', followed by درستست 'is true' and گر نادرست 'or (if) false' — گر is an archaic/poetic conditional/contrastive particle meaning 'if' or introducing the alternative. The clause therefore reads 'whether the speaker is right or wrong'; the linking is tight, with درستستگر forming a compact poetic sequence.
segments.s774-g67: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (3rd sg.). The remainder is a prepositional phrase + verb: بر تخت شاهی نشست = 'sat upon the kingly throne' with بر + تخت 'upon the throne' and شاهی functioning as an adjective or genitival specification 'kingly/kingship'. نشست is past 'sat'. Word order is temporal clause (چو + verb) then the main action.
segments.s774-g68: به افسون خراسانش آمد بدست is an instrumental/causative phrase: به افسون 'by enchantment' (به used instrumentally), خراسانش 'his Khorasan' uses the enclitic -ش (his), آمد 'came' (3rd sg. past), and بدست 'into the hand / into possession' (an idiom meaning 'fell into his hands'). The overall order is somewhat poetic: instrument + theme + motion verb + result.
segments.s774-g69: ببخشید is past tense of بخشیدن; here it functions as 'bestowed' or 'gave' (rather than 'forgave') and takes a following locative phrase: روی زمین بر مهان 'on the ground for the guests' where روی زمین is 'on the ground' and بر مهان indicates the beneficiaries 'for the guests'. The verb is transitive and the exact direct object is implied/omitted (poetic economy).
segments.s774-g70: منم گفت is reported speech: منم 'I too / I am' used as a proclitic reply and گفت is the past verb 'said' (here as speech-report). با مهر is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with kindness/affection' modifying گفت (how he said it). شاه جهان is a title/appositive 'king of the world' identifying the speaker; the sequence is typical Persian: reported quote or utterance marker + manner phrase + appositive title.
segments.s774-g71: جهان را سراسر به بخشش گرفت is structured with جهان را (definite object marked by را) 'the world', سراسر 'entirely / throughout' as an adverbial intensifier, and به بخشش گرفت literally 'took by giving' (= 'won / gained through generosity'). گرفت is the past transitive 'took/ seized'. The construction emphasizes manner ('by generosity') as the means of gaining the world.
segments.s774-g72: ستاره نظاره برو‌ای شگفت is a compact, exclamatory phrase. نظاره برو is an imperative-like cluster: نظاره (view/observation) with برو (imperative 'go'/'turn') used poetically as 'look!' and ای + شگفت is a vocative 'O wonder!' The phrase functions as an interjection calling attention — 'behold the star, O marvel!' Word order is poetic and inverted for emphasis.
segments.s774-g33: همه شب is an adverbial phrase 'the whole night' placed up front. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'because of' governing اندیشه ('thought'). پر precedes the noun and means 'full (of)'; خون بدم is a terse, poetic possession: خون 'blood' followed by بدم as a possessive/relational element ('of me' / 'my blood') without an ezāfe.
segments.s774-g34: جهاندار داند که introduces a reported-clause: 'the sovereign knows that ...'. داند is 3rd-person present/presentive 'knows'. که introduces the subordinate clause; چون here functions as 'how' and governs the following predicate. بدم in this clause is an archaic/personal predicate meaning 'I am / I fare' (not the possessive 'my blood'), a usage not listed among the provided senses.
segments.s774-g35: بدو is the dative 'to him' (prepositional/indirect-object), followed by رای‌زن 'the counsellor' as subject; گفت is being used as the past verb 'said' (the supplied sense list gave nouns, so the actual verbal sense is not listed). که introduces the content reported; اکنون 'now' and گذشت is a past/stative 'has passed' / 'is over'.
segments.s774-g36: ازین is از + این 'from/of this' (here written as a single unit); کار 'affair/deed' names what is meant. گیتی 'the world' is the subject of the predicate پرآواز گشت, where پرآواز ('full of fame' / 'renowned') is a compound adjective and گشت is the past/inchoative 'became'. The clause thus means the affair became known/renowned throughout the world.
segments.s774-g37: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. بازجویی is a verbal-noun 'examination/interrogation' and همی is the classical continuous particle (roughly like modern می‑) marking ongoing action; together they indicate continued scrutiny. کار خویش is a possessive nominal phrase 'your own affair/deed', with خوی + ش forming the possessive 'your'.
segments.s774-g38: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause. بگسستی is the past form 'you broke/severed' (b‑ prefix + past stem), second-person singular. آن is the demonstrative 'that' before the noun رشته 'thread'; تار intensifies/qualifies it as a (thin/delicate) string. خوی + ش again mark possession 'of yours'.
segments.s774-g39: کنون 'now' + او 'he' introduce the subject. بدخمه is a locative noun 'in/into a small dark chamber/sepulchre' and درون خاک 'into the earth' gives the location; شد is the past/inchoative 'became/went' used here as the verb meaning 'entered / was placed'. Word order places locatives before the verb in this poetic style.
segments.s774-g40: روان 'the soul/spirit' with ورا 'to him/for him' (a dative-like clitic) is the logical experiencer. زهر تریاک شد is a nominal predicate with شد 'became' — literally 'became the poison of opium' — i.e. his spirit was afflicted/poisoned by opium. The compact order (object + dative + predicate) is idiomatic in classical Persian.
segments.s774-g1: This is a short narrative clause: کس (indefinite someone) is the subject and آمد is the past tense 'came'. به introduces the person addressed (ماهوی سوری). ماهوی سوری here functions as a proper name plus epithet (Mahuy the Syrian). بگفت is an archaic/simple past reporting verb ('said' / 'told') that leads into reported speech introduced by که.
segments.s774-g2: که introduces the reported clause ('that'). شاه جهان is a title-like compound 'king of the world' (subject). گشت is past of گشتن here meaning 'became/turned' (in the sense of changing state). با خاک جفت is a prepositional phrase 'paired/joined with the earth' (literal 'with earth mate'), an idiomatic poetic way to say he was reduced to dust/buried.
segments.s774-g3: A coordinated list of groups linked by و ('and'). سکوبا is a rare/obscure term (here presented as a group name), followed by قسیس 'priest' and رهبان 'monk/ascetic'. روم postpositively qualifies the last noun, meaning 'of Rome' (Byzantium).
segments.s774-g4: همه quantifies the following plural سوگواران 'mourners' ('all the mourners'). آن مرز و بوم is a fixed locative phrase 'that border and land / that region' which functions as the domain those mourners belong to. Word order is determiner/quantifier + noun + modifier phrase.
segments.s774-g5: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed'. با + مویه 'with lamentation' is an instrumental/ circumstantial phrase describing how they went. برنا و پیر is a paired contrast 'young and old' (both groups participating); the nouns follow the manner phrase as in Persian coordination.
segments.s774-g6: تن شاه is the definite object 'the king's body' (object precedes the verb and is marked by context rather than an overt را here). بردند is past plural 'they carried/took'. زان آبگیر is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from that pond' (زان = 'from that'), indicating the place whence they took the body.
segments.s774-g7: یکی here is the indefinite/partitive 'a certain / one' (introducing a single tomb). دخمه کردند is 'they made/constructed a tomb'. او را is the object with the accusative marker را ('for him'). به باغ uses به to indicate the place/setting ('in/into a garden'), linking where the tomb was made.
segments.s774-g8: بلند و بزرگیش combines an adjective 'tall' and a nominal with a possessive/enclitic -ش ('its height/greatness') — the -ش likely refers back to the باغ (the garden's height/greatness). برتر ز is a comparative construction 'higher/superior than'; ز is the poetic form of 'than/from'. راغ is a comparator (probably a proper name or poetic noun); the whole phrase states that the garden's height and greatness exceeded Rāgh. Note the classical/poetic morphology (enclitic -ش, برتر ز).
segments.s774-g49: تو is the emphatic 2nd‑person singular subject/addressee. این تاج و انگشتری را is the definite direct object (این = 'this', تاج و انگشتری = 'crown and ring', را = object marker). بدار here is an imperative-like exhortation meaning 'hold/keep' (poetic register) addressing the addressee; word order is subject + object + verb, typical in older Persian poetry.
segments.s774-g50: بود introduces an existential/predictive clause ('there will be' / 'there is'), followed by روز کین 'a day of vengeance' (noun + genitive modifier). تاجت آید به کار is a small clause: تاجت ('your crown') is the subject, آید ('will come' / 'will happen') and به کار ('into use' / 'to be of use') form the predicate meaning 'your crown will prove useful' or 'will be needed'. The verb آید is present/subjunctive form used here with future sense.
segments.s774-g51: مرا نیست چیزی جزین در جهان is a classical Persian way to say 'I have nothing in the world except this.' Grammatically مَـرا (to me) plus نیست ('is not / there is not') expresses lack of possession: literally 'to me there is not anything except this in the world.' جزین (= جز + این) limits the negation to 'except this.'
segments.s774-g52: همانا 'verily / truly' is a discourse particle introducing an emphatic clause. که here introduces a content clause 'that ...'. هست این ز تازی نهان = 'this is hidden from the Tazi (Arabs)': هست is the copula/existential 'is', ز (poetic for از) marks source/agent 'from / to', تازی = 'Arab', نهان = 'hidden/secret'. Word order is subject (این) before predicate (ز تازی نهان).
segments.s774-g53: تو زین پس = 'you, henceforth' (زین پس = 'from now on / henceforth'). به دشمن مده is a negative imperative: مده (مِده) = 'do not give' and به دشمن = 'to the enemy'. گاه من follows and literally means 'my time / my place / my occasion' (گاه often = 'time' or 'occasion'); here it marks what should not be handed over—i.e. 'do not give (it) to the enemy in/at my time / my stead.' The phrase shows classical Persian compactness where possession and negation combine (object implied).
segments.s774-g54: نگه‌دار هم is an exhortation 'be the keeper/guardian as well' — نگه‌دار can function as a noun 'guardian' or as an imperative/verb 'keep/watch over' in epic address; هم = 'also'. زین نشان راه من = 'this token/sign of my way' (زین here repeats the demonstrative or echoes 'saddle/sign', نشان = 'token/sign', راه من = 'my path/way/route or cause'). Together: 'also be the keeper of this token of my way.'
segments.s774-g55: من این تاج میراث دارم ز شاه is straightforward: من = 'I', این تاج = 'this crown' (topic/object), میراث دارم = 'I have as an inheritance' (میراث = 'inheritance/heritage', دارم = 'I have'). ز شاه ('from the king') uses poetic ز = از to mark source. Word order places the possessor and possessed in the classical arrangement.
segments.s774-g56: به فرمان او = 'by his command' (به + noun = 'by/on the authority of'). برنشینم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive form used with future sense 'I will sit/ascend (the throne)'; in epic diction برنشستن = 'to be seated (on the throne)'. به گاه = 'at the time / when the occasion comes' (temporal adjunct). The clause thus says he will take the seat when ordered and when the time comes.
segments.s774-g81: چو = temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. لشکر فراوان شد is a simple past/perfective clause 'the army became numerous' (فراوان after the noun is an adjective). شد is the past of شدن 'become'. و links to the next nominal element; خواسته is a noun 'desire/wish' and is left elliptically connected to the following clause (i.e. 'and the heart's desire...').
segments.s774-g82: دل مرد بی تن is a genitival/construct phrase 'the heart of the (bodiless) man' or more literally 'the heart [of] man without a body' (بی modifies مرد). شد آراسته: شد is past 'became' and آراسته (past participle/adjective) functions predicatively 'became adorned'. Word order is noun phrase + predicate.
segments.s774-g83: سپه را: سپه 'army' marked by را as a definite/direct object. درم داد is 'gave dirhams' (داد = gave). و آباد کرد links a second coordinated verb phrase: آباد کردن 'to make prosperous/inhabit' (کرد is the light verb). The subject (he) is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s774-g84: سر دوده خویش is a noun phrase: سر 'head', دوده 'soot/sooty' as qualifier, خوی + ش here render the possessive 'his (own)'. پرباد کرد is a light‑verb construction where پرباد (lit. 'full of wind' / 'windy') is made into a predicate by کرد 'made', i.e. 'caused to be full of wind' or poetically 'blew into/filled with wind'.
segments.s774-g85: به + noun expresses motion/position toward: به آموی شد پهلو پیش رو = 'he became (took position) at/near his paternal uncle's side at the front'. آموی = paternal uncle; شد is past 'became/took up position'; پهلو 'side/flank'; پیش رو here functions as a compound meaning 'in front/forefront'.
segments.s774-g86: ابا لشکری جنگ‌سازان نو is a short noun phrase meaning 'with/an army of new war‑makers'. ابا is archaic/ambiguous here (likely a dialectal or poetic variant related to 'با' = 'with' or a textual irregularity), لشکری 'military/troops' modifies the following plural جنگ‌سازان 'war‑makers/warmongers', and نو 'new' qualifies them. The segment is therefore a circumstantial phrase describing accompaniment.
segments.s774-g87: طلایه is the subject 'vanguard/forefront'. به پیش سپاه = 'to the front of the army' (به + prepositional phrase). اندرون 'inside/within' is an adverbial indicating location. The phrase states positional arrangement: 'the vanguard to the front of the army within'.
segments.s774-g88: جهان‌دیده‌ای is an indefinite adjectival noun 'a world‑seen / worldly / experienced one' (the -ای ending marks indefiniteness/agent). نام او گرستون = 'his name (was) Gorstun' (نام = 'name/reputation'; classical Persian often omits the copula). گرستون here is a proper name; its listed lexical senses ('hunger') do not fit the proper‑name use.
segments.s774-g89: به شهر بخارا نهادند روی is literal word order: prepositional phrase + verb + object. نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ('to place/set') used idiomatically with روی ('face') to mean 'set/turn (their) face toward'. به شهر بخارا = 'toward / to the city of Bukhara'.
segments.s774-g90: چنان ساخته is a participial phrase: ساخته ('made/formed') with چنان ('thus/so') → 'having thus formed'. لشکری جنگجوی is an NP where لشکری (army/troops) and جنگجوی (warrior/war‑like) form 'a warlike army/host'. The whole phrase modifies the subject implied by نهادند.
segments.s774-g91: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو is the dative/clitic 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb). ما را سمرقند و چاچ begins reported speech; ما را here bears the object/contrast marker را with ما to mark the actor in this classical construction (common in older Persian for 'we' in obligation phrases).
segments.s774-g92: بباید گرفتن is the modal + infinitive construction: بباید ('it is necessary / must') + گرفتن ('to take/capture') = 'must capture'. بدین is the old prepositional compound 'ب' + 'دین' = 'by/with this' (instrumental/demonstrative), and مهر و تاج = 'seal and crown' are the instruments/insignia referenced.
segments.s774-g93: به فرمان = 'by the command/decree' (به used adverbially = 'by'). شاه جهان یزدگرد is a title + name apposition: 'the king of the world, Yazdegerd'. Word order is typical: preposition + noun phrase.
segments.s774-g94: که introduces a relative clause 'who…'. سالار (commander) is predicate; the sequence سالار بد او is archaic/inverted: بد here is best read as an archaic/poetic form of بود ('was') so the clause reads 'who was commander (سالار بود او)'. بر این هفت گرد = 'over these seven districts' with بر + noun phrase indicating jurisdiction.
segments.s774-g95: ز بیژن بخواهم: ز = 'from' (poetic), بیژن is the person affected, بخواهم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of خواستن used here with future sense 'I will demand / I will take'. به شمشیر کین uses به (here 'by'/'with') plus instrument شمشیر ('sword') modified by کین ('vengeance') to indicate the means.
segments.s774-g96: کزو = 'from which / by which' (relative pronoun referring back to the thing sought). تیره شد is past tense 'became dark' (تیره = dark/bleak, شد = became). بخت ایران‌زمین = 'the fortune/fate of the land of Iran' — the phrase is the patient affected by the darkening.
segments.s774-g57: بدین here is a fused prepositional expression (به + این) meaning “by/with this”; syntactically it marks the means or instrument. چاره is the noun “remedy/means” and sits immediately after it. بند is used in the sense “stanza/section” and (with an implied numeral ده before it in the phrase) describes the size/form of the composition; بد را is the definite object (را marks بد) and فروغ is the noun 'radiance' that titles or describes the piece. The whole group functions as a noun phrase meaning something like “by this means, (a) ten‑stanza piece, 'Bad's Radiance'.”
segments.s774-g58: The first که introduces the purpose/result clause (“so that/that”), followed by داند (3rd‑person singular present/past narration: “he knows” / “may know”). The second که introduces the content of that knowing (‘that …’). این is the demonstrative “this,” راستست is a fused copular adjective (راست + است) meaning “is true,” and از دروغ “from/of falsehood” completes the opposition “true or false.” Together: “so that he may know whether this is true or false.”
segments.s774-g59: چو + بشنید = a temporal clause “when he heard” (چو = when; بشنید = 3rd‑person past tense “he heard”). ماهوی is the speech‑participant’s name/epithet here (Māhūy) and همین گفتا is the past reporting verb 'said' (گفتا = said); that که begins the quoted content or subordinate clause. زه is an interjection or vocative particle here introducing the speech ('Zah' / 'Oh' / 'I' as an exclamatory address) rather than the literal noun “bow‑string.”
segments.s774-g60: تو دستوری is a predicate phrase: تو = 'you' and دستوری is an adjective meaning 'commanding/imperious' (copula omitted in classical style). و = 'and'; بر تو بر نیست is a doubled prepositional pattern where بر تو = 'over/above you' and بر نیست = 'there is no … upon' (literally 'no … sits upon you'); مه here means 'greatness' or 'eminence' (i.e. no high rank rests on you). The clause as a whole is a critical remark: 'you are imperious, yet no greatness rests on you.'
segments.s774-g61: همه is the universal quantifier 'all' placed before the noun. مهتران (the chiefs/leaders) is the direct object and is marked by را. ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the army' (ز = from, لشکر = army). بَخواند is the past/epic perfect 3rd‑person verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (بخواند used narratively).
segments.s774-g62: وزین گونه is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'in this dignified/weighty manner' (وزین modifies گونه = 'manner, way'). چندین سخنها = 'many/several speeches/words' (چندین = several; سخنها = plural 'words/speeches'). براند is a 3rd‑person past verb from راندن used here figuratively 'he drove forth' = 'he uttered/spoke' those words; the verb carries the action of delivering the speeches.
segments.s774-g63: بدانست is a past tense verb 'knew/realized' (3rd‑person); لشکر is the subject 'the army' and precedes the verb for topical emphasis. که introduces the subordinate clause giving the content of what they understood: این نیست راست = 'this is not true' (این = this; نیست = is not; راست = true).
segments.s774-g64: به شوخی = 'for/by (means of) a jest' or 'for joking' (به = for; شوخی = jest/joking). ورا = 'to him' / 'upon him' (وره = the target). سر بریدن is an infinitival noun phrase 'to cut off the head' (بریدن = to cut; here 'beheading' in classical sense). سزاست is a predicative phrase 'it is deserved/it is fitting' (the copular sense 'is due'). Together: 'for joking, beheading him is the deserved punishment.'
segments.s774-g25: Word order is inverted: بر + noun phrase marks location ('on, upon'); انگشتری is the noun 'ring'; یزدگردست is a fused copular form (یزدگرد + است) meaning 'is Yazdegerd'; نام is the predicative noun 'name'. Together: 'On the ring is the name Yazdegerd.'
segments.s774-g26: به شمشیر introduces instrument/means ('by/with the sword'); بر من is a prepositional phrase 'upon me'; نگردند is negative third-person plural of گرد(یدن)/گشت(ن) 'become/turn' (here used as 'make/turn into'); رام is the adjective 'tame/meek'. Literally: 'By the sword, upon me they do not turn tame' → 'swords will not tame me.'
segments.s774-g27: همه شهر ایران is the topical subject 'all the cities/whole realm of Iran'; ورا ('to him') is the indirect object/beneficiary; بنده بود is the predicate 'were (his) servants' (بنده used predicatively with بود).
segments.s774-g28: اگر ... ار is a conditional frame ('if' ... 'if/ supposing'); خویش (here written split as خوی + ش) means 'his own disposition/character'; بد 'bad/evil'; پراگنده 'scattered/widespread'; بود is past tense used here as the conditional/result ('would have been' / 'was'). The clause means that had his character been bad, things would have become scattered.
segments.s774-g29: نخواند is negative 3rd-person singular/plural of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/name' (as in 'call someone something'). The clause is inverted: مرد داننده (a 'knowing' or wise man) is the agent/subject, مرا (me — split here as م + را) is the direct object, and شاه is the predicative complement ('king') — so: 'A knowing man would not call me king.'
segments.s774-g30: نه begins a negation 'nor'; بر مهرم is a prepositional phrase 'on/for my مهر' (here 'مهر' = favour/affection or seal), آرام گیرد is a verbal phrase 'take rest / become calm' (گیرد = 'take/receive' used with آرام), and سپاه 'army' is the grammatical subject placed at the line end by poetic inversion. Together: 'nor will the army find rest on my behalf.'
segments.s774-g31: جزین 'except this' sets exclusivity; بود is past 'there was'; چاره 'remedy/means' is the predicate noun; مرا ('for me' split as م + را) marks whose remedy it is; در نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret/hiddenly'. The sense: 'There was no remedy for me except this, in secret.'
segments.s774-g32: چرا is the question word 'why'; ریختم is first-person past of ریختن 'I poured/shed' (here 'shed' blood); خون 'blood'; شاه جهان is a title-like genitive 'the king of the world' (شاه modified by جهان). Literally: 'Why did I shed the blood of the king of the world?'
segments.s774-g73: به introduces the recipient ('to'); مهتر پسر is the recipient noun phrase 'the chief's son' (مهتر = chief/elder, پسر = son). داد is the past-tense transitive verb 'gave'; in this poetic word order the verb precedes the direct objects (بلخ و هری) which follow it.
segments.s774-g74: فرستاد is the past tense 'sent/dispatched' (here purposeful sending). بر + هر سوی is a directional phrase 'toward every direction' (بر takes a directional sense), and لشکری is a collective object 'troops/an army' (indefinite/collective). Word order places the verb first, then the directional phrase and the object.
segments.s774-g75: بداندیشگان is the plural noun 'ill‑wishers/malefactors' as the direct object; را is the definite/object marker; همه is the quantifier 'all/everyone'; برکشید is a past transitive verb 'lifted up/removed' used idiomatically here to mean 'uprooted/put down'.
segments.s774-g76: بدانسان here functions adverbially 'in that way' (not as 'bad person'); که introduces a relative/explanatory clause; از گوهر او literally 'from/of his essence' (گوهر = essence/inner quality, او = his); سزید is past/impersonal 'was fitting/was deserved' (i.e. 'befitted him' or 'was appropriate'). The clause says the action was done in a manner fitting his nature.
segments.s774-g77: بدان here is the demonstrative 'to that man/that one' (formed from ب + دان); را is the object marker. بهر جای is a fixed phrase 'for the place/post' meaning 'to the office' (بهر = for/toward, جای = place/post). سالار کرد = 'made (him) commander' (کرد = made). The whole segment means 'he made that man commander in that post.'
segments.s774-g78: خردمند را: 'the wise man' as definite object (را). سرنگونسار is a compound meaning 'overturning/toppling' or 'one who overthrows'; followed by کرد ('made/did') it functions as a verbal phrase 'he overthrew/deposed (the) wise man.' The construction is causative/resultative: he caused the wise man to be cast down.
segments.s774-g79: چو 'when' begins a temporal clause. زیر اندر آمد literally 'the under/undermining came in' — here زیر is used metaphorically for subversion/underhand conduct and اندر آمد = 'entered/came in'. سر راستی is a compound meaning 'uprightness/honesty' (سر intensifies/heads the quality). The clause sets up a causal/result scene: when undermining came in...
segments.s774-g80: پدید آمد = 'appeared/was revealed' (past); از هر سوی = 'from every side' (از = from, هر سوی = every direction); کاستی = 'shortcoming/deficiency'. Together: 'shortcomings appeared from every side.' The verb-predicate follows the temporal clause to report the visible result.
segments.s774-g17: همان تاج = 'that same crown' (determiner + noun). با او بد و مهر شاه is a compact, slightly archaic phrase: با او = 'with him/for him'; بد here is a poetic spelling/short form of باد (optative 'may it be') and so should be read as an optative particle (see senses note); و مهر شاه = 'and the king's favour'. Grammatically this whole chunk is the object wished for (see the following verb آرزو کرد).
segments.s774-g18: شبان‌زاده را آرزو کرد = literal order 'shepherd‑son (obj) wished (he) did': آرزو (noun) + کرد (past auxiliary) form the compound 'آرزو کرد' = 'he wished/desired'; را marks شبان‌زاده as the beneficiary/indirect object ('for the shepherd's son'). گاه at the end is an adverbial 'at that time/then'.
segments.s774-g19: همه رازدارانش را پیش خواند = 'he called/summoned all his confidants before [him]'. رازدارانش is a plural with the possessive suffix -ش 'his'; را marks the direct object; پیش خواند = 'called before (him)', where خواند is past tense 'called/summoned' and پیش indicates presence/in front of.
segments.s774-g20: سخن هرچ بودش به دل در براند is a purpose/result clause: سخن هرچ بودش = 'whatever words there were (he had)'; بودش is بود + enclitic ش 'it/they were (his)'. به دل = 'into/within the heart' and در براند is a causative/transitive use of راندن 'to drive/put', meaning 'to place/keep in the heart'. Together: he summoned them so that his words would be lodged in their hearts.
segments.s774-g21: به دستور = 'by (or at) the order/command' (به used here causally/instrumentally). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses list does not show the simple 'say' sense, so sense index is set to -1). ای جهاندیده مرد is a vocative phrase 'O worldly-wise man!' addressed by the speaker.
segments.s774-g22: فراز آمد آن روز ننگ و نبرد = 'there rose/appeared that day of shame and battle.' فراز آمد is an inverted order (familiar in poetry) meaning 'rose/appeared on high' or simply 'arose'; آن روز = 'that day'; ننگ و نبرد = 'shame and battle' (a compound naming the calamitous day).
segments.s774-g23: نه گنجست با من نه نام و نژاد = a paired negation: 'neither is there treasure with me, nor [is there] name and lineage.' گنجست is an older/poetic form meaning 'there is treasure' (here negated). با من = 'with me'; نام و نژاد = 'name (fame) and lineage'.
segments.s774-g24: همی داد خواهم سر خود بباد uses an older word order and optative phrasing: همی (emphatic 'indeed' / poetic progressive), داد خواهم is inverted compared with Modern Persian خواهم داد and means 'I will give', سر خود = 'my head' (literally 'head of myself'), and بباد is an optative 'be to the wind / be blown away' — idiomatically 'I will give up my head' or 'I will risk my life'.

sentences.s774-s5: Thus spoke the ill‑fated, ill‑omened Mahuy: "That Iran did not exist before Rome itself."
sentences.s774-s6: He sent (men) so that whoever had made that tomb—
sentences.s774-s7: They killed and plundered the frontier; such were Mahuy's desire and wealth.
sentences.s774-s8: After that, go about the world and look; from the seed of the great ones he saw no one.
sentences.s774-s21: Gather all the worldly-wise men; sharpen your tongue with sweet speech.
sentences.s774-s22: Say this: that the king gave me this crown and ring for the sake of favor/preeminence.
sentences.s774-s23: When he learned that a host was coming from the Turks, and when the night grew darker, the king summoned me.
sentences.s774-s24: He told me: when the wind of battle rose — who in the world knows upon whom it will fall?
sentences.s774-s33: One champion said, "This affair is a test; the speaker's words may be true or false."
sentences.s774-s34: When he heard (it), he sat upon the kingly throne; by enchantment Khorasan came into his hands.
sentences.s774-s35: He bestowed (a place) on the ground for the guests; "I too," he said, with the kingly kindness.
sentences.s774-s36: He took the whole world by generosity; behold the star — O wonder!
sentences.s774-s17: All night I was full of blood from thought; the sovereign knows how I am.
sentences.s774-s18: The counsellor said to him, 'Now it is past' — for this affair the world became famous.
sentences.s774-s19: Now you still question/examine your own deed, because you have broken that thread, that string of yours.
sentences.s774-s20: Now he went into the small tomb, into the earth; his soul to him became the poison of opium.
sentences.s774-s1: Someone came to Mahuy the Syrian and said that the king of the world had been joined with the earth (turned to dust).
sentences.s774-s2: Sekubā, priests, and monks of Rome — all were mourners of that borderland.
sentences.s774-s3: They departed with lamentation — young and old — and carried the king's body from that pond.
sentences.s774-s4: They made him a tomb in a tall garden, whose height and greatness surpassed Rāgh.
sentences.s774-s25: Keep this crown and ring; a day of vengeance will come when your crown will be of use.
sentences.s774-s26: I have nothing in the world except this; truly, this is hidden from the Arabs.
sentences.s774-s27: From now on do not give (my) saddle to the enemy; also be the guardian of this token of my way.
sentences.s774-s28: I possess this crown as an inheritance from the king; at his command I will be seated (upon it) when the time comes.
sentences.s774-s41: When the army grew numerous and the heart's desire was present, the heart of the bodiless man became adorned.
sentences.s774-s42: He gave the army dirhams and made (things) prosperous; he made his soot‑blackened head full of wind.
sentences.s774-s43: He took his place at his paternal uncle's side at the front, with an army of newly‑formed war‑makers.
sentences.s774-s44: He placed the vanguard before the army within; a worldly‑seasoned man named Gorstun.
sentences.s774-s45: They turned their faces toward the city of Bukhara, having thus formed a warlike host.
sentences.s774-s46: He said to him, 'We must capture Samarkand and Chach with this seal and crown.'
sentences.s774-s47: By the command of the king of the world, Yazdegerd, who was commander over these seven districts.
sentences.s774-s48: I will take from Bijan, by the sword of vengeance, that which has darkened the fortune of the land of Iran.
sentences.s774-s29: By this means (or remedy) prepare/compose a ten‑stanza (بندِ ده) piece called 'Bad's radiance', so that he may know whether this is truth or falsehood.
sentences.s774-s30: When Māhūy heard, he said: 'Zah, you are commanding, and no greatness rests upon you.'
sentences.s774-s31: He summoned all the chiefs from the army, and in this dignified manner spoke many speeches (uttered many weighty words).
sentences.s774-s32: The army realized that this was not true; for a jest, to behead him is the fitting/deserved punishment.
sentences.s774-s13: The ring bears the name Yazdegerd; swords will not make me tame.
sentences.s774-s14: All the cities of Iran were his subjects; if his nature had been evil, they would have been scattered.
sentences.s774-s15: A knowing man would not call me king; nor will the army rest for my sake.
sentences.s774-s16: There was no remedy for me except this, hidden away; why did I spill the blood of the king of the world?
sentences.s774-s37: He gave Balkh and Harī to the chief's son, and sent troops in every direction.
sentences.s774-s38: He removed/uprooted all the ill-wishers, in such a manner as befitted his nature.
sentences.s774-s39: He appointed that man to the post of commander, and he deposed the wise man.
sentences.s774-s40: When subversion entered, uprightness was revealed and shortcomings appeared on every side.
sentences.s774-s9: At that time he wished that the same crown and the king's favour be with the shepherd's son.
sentences.s774-s10: He summoned all his confidants before him, so that whatever words there were he might put into (their) hearts.
sentences.s774-s11: By command he said, 'O worldly-wise man!' — then came that day of shame and battle.
sentences.s774-s12: I have neither treasure nor renown and lineage; indeed I will give my head to the wind.
